item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: None of the images could be verified to be compliant with CC 3.0. They have all since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1, I like this one. Good writing and images.

Typo:

SCP-1913-3 appears to be adolescent, male black Labrador retriever (Canis lupus familiaris), lacking a mouth, nose and eyes.

item.title: image source
item.content:encoded:
no idea about Freddie, but Agatha is someone's repurposed art project - "Ceramic Cat with Corpse Paint". There's color versions on Pinterest, pointing to a (dead) eBay store, which i assume means someone tried to sell this.

There's no indication that it's available under a compatible license.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In the words of Rodger Bumpass, "Okay, new theory."

So if SCP-2999-A isn't Clovis, then what the fuck is it? Similarly to the last post, I have theorized two possible situations.

Number one, SCP-2999-A is trying to find Clovis, whether it be to destroy her, or simply because no one knows where she is. I mean there are a few characters that we have been introduced to in Pitch Haven, but haven't really showed up since. Characters that may come to mind include Espin, Huesic, and Jacquelyn. Because we do not know a lot about these characters, they could have innumerable possible goals and motives. As SCP-2999-A has a file relating to Clovis hidden somewhere deep within its hard drive, it obviously has something to do with her, even if the object itself is not directly related to her. This being said, SCP-2999-A may be or may have been created by a completely separate entity who is using the gathered information as a means to track her down. I'm going to go ahead and point the finger at Agatha since she is, after all, the founder of Prometheus Labs. But hey, it could be anybody… It could even be me! That would be one hell of a plot twist!

Theory two is that SCP-2999-A was created by Clovis, but the project ultimately fucked up in the end. So SCP-2999-A's goal seems to be to resurrect the former denizens of Eden, or at least the Crafters. Clovis' goal seems to be help those in need. Short and sweet. Anyway, the two's motives go hand in hand when you really think about it. Two-Triple-Nine (that's what I'm calling it for now on, don't judge me) revives the dead, and Clovis helps them recover from the trauma caused by the Nachash event. However, Two-Triple-Nine, at least as of currently, seems to have fallen flat on its face. I sure do hope someone got fired for that blunder!

Anyway, that's just my opinion. Really, at this point I'm just waiting for Fantem to come out with a new entry for the series. Fuck, for all I know, Clovis could be the physical manifestation of man's inner struggle against traffic laws. So yeah…

Fan theories aside, if you look on the Pitch Haven hub, you can still see on Clovis' PoI file that she exists as both SCP-1913-2 and another entity. The identity of this entity is unknown, likely because Fantem hasn't yet gotten around to writing the article, but still. Guess that's something you can look forward to.

Finally, I don't mind a bit of criticism on the theory. It's no harm done, really. I just wanted to share my opinion, and if somebody's opinion opposes mine, they have the right to say so. I don't want this to be taken as a "no, you are wrong," but rather a "this is news to me. What now?" Anyway, if I make this post any longer, the almighty God of collapsibles is going to have my head, so that just about wraps it up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not to critique your theory or anything, but after "No Safe Haven" was released, the creator of the pitch haven series (Fantem) confirmed that SCP-2999-A is NOT Clovis. So we really don't know where she is right now in the series…sad too, because I really want to see more of her! :,(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, Clovis currently seems to be incarnated as, or at least taking physical possession of, SCP-2999-A. This can mean one of two things.

The first, and most likely theory is that SCP-1913-2 is Clovis' real body, and Clovis' sentience has been temporarily transferred to SCP-2999-A. This roughly means that SCP-1913-2 is just being cared for by Frederick until Clovis has use for it again. Quite similarly to how Frederick's sentience was, quite literally, chained to a desk in "No Safe Haven," while his actual body just wandered around aimlessly until Father Morgan moved it aside to use it to make his Wolf's Pitch… Er… Something to that effect. It is quite possible that Agatha and Espen work in a similar manor to this, Agatha existing as both SCP-1913-1 and Agatha Wight, and Espen existing as both the stuffed bear and… Well, Espen.

The second theory is that Clovis and "Telly" are two separate entities. It is slightly offsetting that Clovis is described as being more wolf-like than rabbit-like in this article. And how did Frederick even come up with the name "Telly?" It's like he just pulled the name out of his ass one day and said, "Ay. Skidaddle, skidooddle. You're fuckin' Telly now!"

Uhg… God, where's an interrobang when you need one?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article makes me wonder: The last time we saw Freddie (chronologically) was in "No Safe Haven" when he and Clovis went off to do…something. Now cut to this SCP and it raises a lot of questions. Particularly, what happened to Clovis? At first I thought SCP-1913-2 was Clovis until I read it's description and thought: "Huh. That's not like Clovis at all". So what happened to her? She…didn't die…did she? Now I'm all worried!

item.title: Wikiwalk Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
edit: edits made.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ Show edit
- Hide

To link to other content featuring Hayward, I propose the following crosslink:

Dr. Hayward was released from intensive care one month after this interview, recovering from third degree burns over his arms and torso, and the cauterization of a hole through his chest.

to

Dr. Hayward was released from intensive care one month after this interview, recovering from third degree burns over his arms and torso, and the cauterization of a hole through his chest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've only got one bit of beef with this article.

Fires started due to contact with these flames will continue to burn until a subject's skin has been fully consumed, or until the fire has been put out.

Fires…act like fires until someone stops them from acting like fires? This phrasing just suggests that it's a pretty normal fire.

That said, I really do like the style behind this one, although I do feel that the 'hook' on it is a little weak/unexplored. Potential there for more, but a +1 either way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry about responding to an old article, but is the fluid coming from 1913-1 related to the paint from 1619-1?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, yeah. 1903's pretty overt about its effect on him, though. This is… subtler. I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This, and also, given that he's kinda… not really human, he didn't actually need it to begin with. He was also set on fire (in addition to what happened here), had his neck snapped, twice, was shot, had fallen off of really high things, stabbed, really stabbed, and poisoned.

Basically, like all of the main characters/his kind, very hard to destroy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1913-2 will attack its victims when provoked, typically through clawing at the victim. However, despite major organ damage and blood loss, SCP-1913-2 is not capable of killing a victim. Subjects will show continued life signs until sustaining fatal injuries from another source, including non-anomalous wounds and the effects of SCP-1913-1's ink. If an organ or limb has been separated from the subject, then that organ will continue to live independently from the subject. Subjects will not die if an organ separated from its body via SCP-1913-2 dies.

It's not the first or last anomalous thing to happen to him, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought that was pretty clear.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is it just me, or does this strongly imply that Hayward just kinda recovered from the attack and then lived the whole rest of his life with his heart literally missing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kind of liked it, but -1.
I had to read it a second time to get what felt off about it. While it IS cool that you set it prior to 1920, it feels off. The idea of a young Foundation having a fleet of Model-Ts (by far the least conspicuous and most affordable brand of the day) seems logistically unlikely, and a Caravan of Ford Model T's coming through town would have taken amnestics for locals to forget. That being said, if you embraced it, those chases could be historically awesome. Perhaps the first caravan encounter was in an armored horse-drawn wagon? This is less an actual anachronism, and more a little off.
The actual anachronism, which could be addressed, is the use of tranq darts in encounter 15. The first (recorded) modern tranq darts were prototyped in the 1950s. The modern Foundation (to varying degree by headcanon) has technology in advance of that used by the public, but the younger Foundation seems like it should have had less of a head-start, and the tranquilizer dart seems to be a good candidate for leaking to the public. If you removed the tranq darts, or (better) addressed their requisition from the Foundation quartermaster, it would tidy up the anachronism.

Yes, very nitpicky… on second thought … no vote, and a potential for a strong +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it. Quite unsettling, and leaves me yearning to know more. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just rechecked this, saw the new picture. At first I was disappointed, because it didn't have the impact of the original. But when one right-clicks and selects 'view image'..

Fuck that. Jesus Christ.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I've fixed the issues you've mentioned. I think it looks a lot better overall now. Thanks for the help!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmmm. I feel like there's a core concept behind this that I would enjoy if done right, but as-is I have a few issues with the presentation that, to be honest, kind of put a damper on the article overall for me.

First of all, your writing style is pretty adverb-heavy. I'm not against adverbs on principle, but I feel like you use more than are necessary and it kind of weighs down your sentences.

Second, there's a lot of awkward wording in here. I'll go through and try to point it out.

the avoidance and clean up of SCP-1913-2 and SCP-1913-3

SCP-1913-2 and -3 are not the things being cleaned up here, but the phrasing makes it seem as if they are. Cleaned up after, but not cleaned up themselves. Second, I think it's clean-up when used as a noun? But I'm not sure.

All SCP-1913 instances are to be placed in the middle of a tubular, 5 cm thick glass ring containing sulfur dust, with a radius of 4 m.

Wording suggests they're all in the same ring. I recommend "Each SCP-1913 instance is […]" to make that a lot more clear here. In addition, the order in which information is presented here is a bit strange, going from dimensions of ring to constituents of glass but then back to dimensions of the ring. In addition, the wording here makes it hard for me to tell if the dust is supposed to be in the glass or just scattered inside the ring.

SCP-1913 instances are to remain within the perimeter of their respective rings indefinitely. The area outside of each ring represents a 'safe zone'.

"Safe zone" isn't really a very concrete, scientific way to word it. What is that supposed to mean? In addition, since you've already set up the rings as a containment device in the previous paragraph, I'm not sure it's necessary to affirm that the objects should not leave them. Overall this paragraph feels kind of unneeded.

Staff must remove any visible armor, and lab coats before entering SCP-1913-2's cell.

I think that comma is incorrect? Also, the paragraphs before this one in the containment procedures all have one unifying topic, but this paragraph is kind of… strangely structured. It presents a bunch of information without a common subject, and does so in a somewhat inexplicable order.

SCP-1913 instances do not show signs of mortality, either regenerating typically fatal injuries overtime, or reappearing near the place of 'death' when their body has been destroyed within an hour. All SCP-1913 instances have shown an extreme aversion to sulfur, being either unwilling or unable to touch or cross over it without assistance.

I feel like the fact that this is the first information presented about the entities in the description is a bit odd. It's important information to convey, and I get the idea of mentioning what they all share in common before talking about each one individually, but it's an… unusual note to start on, and I feel like these details give the description kind of a slow start. Also, "overtime" should definitely be "over time" instead. Also the use of the singular "they" here is weirdly humanizing, and I'd suggest you either go with straight-up plural or "it" to refer to the object.

and a black, wet ink-like substance

I think you need another comma after "wet"?

emanating a young female voice from its interior

This phrasing really bothers me and I'm not entirely sure why. I feel like "from its interior" is a bit redundant; knowing that the voice comes from the object itself, there aren't many other places it can be emanating from, if that makes sense.

but has also been known to withhold or give false information relevant to its own containment, such as its detestation of sulfur.

The sentence structure here implies that "withhold" is meant to apply to the subject "information" later in the sentence, but it really can't because "give false information" is one complete verb phrase here, not a noun. So you can withhold information and give false information, or you can withhold and falsify information, but you can't withhold and give false information. Additionally, I feel like you mean to insinuate that it withheld its dislike of sulfur from researchers, but since there are two verbs in the first part of the sentence you really need to specify which one you mean because as-is it's very confusingly worded.

Depending on the quantity of ink, the affected areas will begin to dissolve, and eventually disappear.

…What depends on the quantity of ink? How fast they dissolve, how much dissolves, what? You need to specify.

If the torso has been consumed, but the subjects' limbs remain,

The whole paragraph before this refers to a singular subject, so the sudden change to plural here is jarring.

does not appear to be capable of spreading on to objects,

Should be "onto," I think.

those who haven't touched SCP-1913-1

I'm not sure what site policy is on contractions, but they're generally considered unsuited for a formal document, if I recall correctly, so…

only capable of causing the disappearance of living tissue

Define living tissue. This bothers me, because then wouldn't things like fingernails and hair, which are technically not 'alive' remain? If so, that would certainly be worth mentioning.

Washing the substance off completely upon contact is sufficient to prevent its effects, though scarring may occur.

How long is the threshold before washing it off doesn't work? How quickly does it absorb? Given how many other details are given in the article, the exclusion of this seems strange.

I'll edit the rest of my commentary into this post later because I have neither the time nor the energy for this right now. Aside from the phrasing, though, I feel like this article focuses too much on being scary/creepy and not enough on being interesting, though that's just personal preference. In addition, it's very long and there isn't enough payoff for that length, in my opinion. I like a lot of long SCPs, but I feel like the length in this one doesn't include enough interesting details to make that really worth it. So, I'm downvoting, for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fixed. Glad you enjoyed, and thanks a bunch! I had no idea how else to phrase the fatal injuries bit.

However, those last two were intentional, especially the second one (must've missed it up thread). Since the entities are firm believers of the 6th, the foundation would have been the ones to finish off Dr. ██████.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's some awkward phrasing here and there, also typos.

regarding other SCP-1913 instances' abilities and reasons for pursuit

but the subject's limbs remain

There might be more that I haven't noticed.

Subjects will show continued life signs until another method presents itself (for example, a knife to the heart or a gunshot to the stomach).

This is super awkward and also doesn't explain the effect well enough. Would the subject still die from a knife to the heart if his heart was completely removed by -2? Would a subject who received a fatal wound from -2 die if he receives a minor injury from another source? Would something like that work instead as a description: "Subjects will show continued life signs until sustaining [fatal?] damage from another source, including non-anomalous wounds, injuries caused by SCP-1913-3, and the effects of SCP-1913-1's ink" ?

SCP-1913 instances do not show signs of mortality.

I don't like this because it's super vague. Are they impervious to any damage? Do they regenerate? Do they just keep on going regardless of any damage done to them? Do they rematerialize from thin air whenever their bodies are completely destroyed? I'd prefer mortality to be replaced with what's actually happening.

There's also some ambiguous phrasing in the encounter reports that I don't like. For example:

Upon arrival at Site ██ on 04/02/191█, SCP-1913-2 and SCP-1913-3 attacked.

Whose arrival? Attacked whom?

which caused considerable damage to the vehicle before escaping.

I think you mean that -2 and -3 damaged the vehicle and then escaped, but it reads as if they damaged the vehicle before the vehicle escaped.

while preventing Dr. ██████'s termination.

Someone mentioned it earlier in the discussion, but I'll reiterate. Termination is something the Foundation does to people, it's authorized by higher-ups. "while preventing Dr. ██████ from being killed by the entities." would be more appropriate.

Good skip. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not really.

Definition of MOTILE
- exhibiting or capable of movement

First rule of a site like this: check any words you don't know and think might be wrong.

Edit: Should be mobile: not really, this is damn creepy: yes really.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
despite lacking the tissues normally required for motility.

Should be mobility.

Also, this is damn creepy. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mythology reference, generally well-done, and creepy as heck pictures? +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To be honest, i don't really get what's going on behind the scenes here, but this is fantastically done nonetheless.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No hard feelings. At least you didn't go all the way and start calling them by names and stuff. It's just there and I can live with it. There's a lot I love about this article, as I said.

For example, that it is actually shown how they figured out the sulfur aversion bit. That's a nice way of adding to the narrative. :]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alright, fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, the Photoshopped face is a lot less obvious on that one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really? -1 reminds me more of el Día de Muertos. I don't think Zalgo ever utilized the cross of St. Peter. As for three… would this work better?

http://scp-wiki.wdfiles.com/local--files/scp-1913/FreddieAlt.jpg (Link now dead)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Am I the only one who doesn't like the -1 and -3 pictures? (-2 is genuinely scary.) -1 looks like Zalgo Cat! and -3 a bad Photoshop job.

item.title: I added a few things
item.content:encoded:
In the spirit of maybe making it all more/less clear (I don't know which), I've edited it up a bit. Specifically the closing statement and the part about Agatha giving information. I really want this to pass the quota I set for myself, and have it be viewed better than it already is, so maybe this makes it okay.

So, better? Worse? Pretty much the same, and I missed the point entirely?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Noooooooooo don't delete it.

You can add a few things, and you can always revert if that doesn't work out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I fixed the majority of the appearances, said 'does not require substance for survival', and switched it back to mortal. Names are there for a reason though. They weren't there for mystique, it's just cause I like giving things names that reflect their place. Plus, if something goes by a name, I'd like to think the Foundation would mention it somewhere. It's more relevant than whether someone has black or blonde hair, because you work with them every other day, and would know this.

Their name isn't always apparent, and if this information isn't mentioned, almost no one would question the phrase "How about we go get Freddie, and paint the town red?" except maybe the two people who looked into it. Even prisons care about prisoners original names. Granted, they don't call them by those names, but they understand the significance.

Anyway, that's just me having the exact opposite peeve. I'm glad you enjoyed regardless. =3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Am I the only one who thinks Freddie's 'missing' face is now…well, Telly's face?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No, it's just a ring of sulfuric encased in a glass tube 5 cm thick. The radius of the ring is 4m, and the entity is in the middle of it. Lemme see if I can make it more clear.

E: Maybe now?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was very good to read this post after I was considering to delete the article so I work on it more. Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All SCP-1913 instances are to be placed in the middle of a ring of sulfur dust encased in a glass tube at least 5 cm thick with a radius of 4m.

This line feels ambiguous. Is the instance encased in a glass tube and also surrounded by sulfur dust? Is the sulfur dust encased in a glass tube? Is the inside diameter of the tube 5cm, or is that the thickness of the glass?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Furious Furries

I actually do like the title, it makes sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh, I just had no idea what to call the thing. Roget's suggestion seemed to fit the best for three reasons:

1. They are both three entities
2. It's ONE R away from being furries, which is what they technically are, sapient/humanoid beings based off animals.
3. They both have a thing for vengeance.

Besides, Freddie's a dude.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I suggested it because it was three entities and they were angry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh god, Freddie gon' give me nightmares. Been a while since we had a skip with this much horrifying imagery.

I'm fascinated by the idea that -2 can't actually kill things, while -1's ink has such a horrifying effect. Really, it's just neat to see something get downgraded from Keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1913 - The Furies

It's not so much based on a myth…
- Fantem

*Stares with suspicion* >.>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You used the name I suggested :D

Good dialogue and a good skip. Take a well-deserved upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Another thing for me to have nightmares about. Take your upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fantem, I think I've figured out why I like your SCPs. It's not because your tone is good and your technical writing is excellent. It's not because you set them in different eras, which gives the Foundation a different feel. It's not that your pictures play with the mind and make me want to see more. All of those are good things, but they're not what win me over.

It's because your SCPs are, quite simply, scary. Scary in the way creepypasta should be. Your articles hearken back to our 4chan roots, while still holding up to modern site standards remarkably well. This is short, scary, bizarre, inexplicable, and utterly fascinating.

Take your +1, and keep doing what you're doing. You're good at it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thats pretty scary o.0
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Damn, this is very interesting. The pictures match, it manages to mix lots of different object varieties and it actually has a narrative. I won't dismiss it on the grounds that I don't understand it all fully, because that happens, and probably one day I'll have that sudden snap and then forego sleeping for a few days.

Some things, though:

SCP-1913 instances do not appear capable of dying

They're not really shown to be alive, though. Maybe pedantic, but sometimes I can't help it.

SCP-1913-2's skeleton appears to be human in structure, with the exception of its skull and digits, which appear to belong to a large canine.

The pesky "appear to" thing. It's starting to get to me, now.

Although SCP-1913-2 does not require substance, it has shown a proclivity towards eating the meat of its victims, and will become agitated if food is not provided.

Then she does need substance. If only as a craving instead of actual biological necessity, but still.

Finally, given names and codenames are also starting to become a peeve of mine. It doesn't ruin the SCP, but it suddenly halts my enthusiasm, except when it actually means anything. In this case, it seems to just be there to be mysteeeeerious.

Nitpicks aside, it got me hooked. Marvelous.

EDIT: Wait…

*sees the entry in the list* *reads title*

AW, F*CK! O_O

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The changes you made to the draft really helped! +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not so much based on a myth, as it 'Follows the laws and symbolisms that the people who made the myths believed.' Basically, they would have fit in the myth or theology, but they were never there IRL.

I have mentioned that they're doing this for family matters, and the probable maturity of Agatha though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Perhaps I'm just being ignorant or perhaps I'm just looking for something that isn't there to 'get' in the first place.

All I get from this skip is these things are strange and do things for some reason.

There seems to be no point or explanation or origination or anything like that, unless this is based on some myth or another that I'm unfamiliar with, and that would be cause for a downvote from me anyway. It's a shame really because it's well-written and seems as if it could be something that we'd all love to get behind because it's fresh and you've managed to create three interesting skips in one, but interesting alone isn't always enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, I suppose you're right. Hold on, I'll see what I can do when I get to a computer.

E: I think it's better now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Over all, I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That doesn't negate the reader's point, though. It does muddle the writing a bit and make it harder to understand what is happening.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1913-1 has the name 'Agatha' etched on the bottom, and is decorated with white gloss paint on the nose, ears, and forehead, and a black, wet ink-like substance around the object's eyes, mouth, and paws, designated SCP-1913-A.

I did discuss the possibility of a fourth entity though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, you said there were three entities, but you stated four, SCP-1913-1 through 3, and scp-1913-A. I'm a bit confused.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The images! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I enjoyed it. It certainly leaves me wanting more information, but I've never been the best at picking up subtleties. I'm sure there is some deeper meaning, some backstory that I can't see. Guess I'll have to wait till you clear it up or someone else figures it out and posts it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Explain to me how 'mortal' is not an appropriate word to use?

From Merriam-Webster:

mor·tal
a : subject to death <mortal man>

It doesn't denote emotion, personal taste or any other form of subjectiveness.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really? I don't think I left anything out that wasn't intended/could be figured out on your own. Could you elaborate?

Also, people I showed it to seemed confused on how whether or not they could die when they read it without that.
